title: Installing a shower flow reducer
subtitle: A five-minute fitting that cuts shower water use by a third
excerpt: A flow reducer inserted into the shower hose or head connection limits water throughput to 6-8 litres per minute without affecting water pressure noticeably. A standard shower runs at 10-15 litres per minute. The reducer cuts that by 30-50 percent.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK guidance on shower flow rates and flow restrictor fitting. Consumer Council for Water shower water consumption data. Grohe and Hansgrohe technical notes on flow restrictors in hand showers.

content.text: A shower flow reducer is a small plastic disc or washer with a calibrated hole. It fits between the shower hose and the shower head, or between the hose and the wall outlet. The restrictor reduces the maximum flow rate without reducing the water pressure, so the spray pattern remains full. Flow reducers are rated in litres per minute and are available in 6, 7, 8, and 9 litre-per-minute versions.

Before fitting a reducer, measure the current flow rate. Hold a bucket under the shower head for exactly 10 seconds with the shower running at normal temperature. Multiply the collected volume in litres by 6 to get litres per minute. A typical UK shower runs at 10-15 litres per minute. Above 10 litres per minute, a flow reducer will make a measurable difference. Below 8 litres per minute the shower may already have a restrictor or may have low mains pressure.

Unscrew the hose from the shower head. Most connections are hand-tight plus a quarter turn; if the fitting is corroded, use an adjustable spanner with a cloth wrapped around the nut to avoid scratching the finish.
Remove the existing rubber washer from inside the fitting. Note its thickness.
Insert the flow reducer disc into the fitting, then replace the rubber washer on top of it.
Reconnect the hose to the shower head. Hand-tighten firmly; the rubber washer provides the seal without requiring tools.
Turn on the shower and check for leaks at the connection. If the fitting seeps, tighten a quarter-turn more.

Start with an 8-litre-per-minute reducer. This reduces a 12-litre shower to 8 litres while maintaining a spray pattern most users find acceptable. A 6-litre reducer cuts further but produces a noticeably thinner spray; it is more suitable for low-pressure properties or for users who are highly motivated to minimise water use. Properties with a mains pressure below 1 bar should not use a reducer below 8 litres per minute as the shower may not function correctly.

Cutting shower flow from 12 to 8 litres per minute saves 4 litres per minute. For a household average of two eight-minute showers per day, that is 64 litres per day saved, or around 23,000 litres per year. Because shower water is heated, cutting the flow also cuts the energy used to heat it by roughly the same proportion.
